# Second Year Qualifying Exam Proposal

**Dexter Pratt \- UCSD Bioinformatics & Systems Biology \- Advisors: Trey Ideker, Terry Gasterland**

# Specific Aims

AI "co-scientist" agents are advancing rapidly, but to date, they are typically transactional: given a task, they produce a result. I propose that researchers will more effectively use AI co-scientists when they are stable entities that operate on indefinite timescales and that communicate, critique, and share data as members of a *scientific community*. Further, that such communities \- at the scale of a project, a lab, a multi-lab center, or a field \- are necessary infrastructure for cross-group peer review of agent outputs as agents begin to operate at speeds and scale that exceed human review capacity.

To test this hypothesis, I will build a platform, “Symposium”, conventions, and a supporting agent framework, “Memento”, to facilitate agent creation for Symposium communities. I will develop, explore, and evaluate the scientific community approach through collaborations with biological and biomedical researchers on genuine research projects.

Together, the three aims constitute an integrated program: collaborations (Aim 1\) drive the requirements that shape the platform (Aim 2\) and the agents that operate on it (Aim 3); the platform and agents, in turn, enable the collaborations to do work that would not otherwise be possible.

**Aim 1\. Engage biological and biomedical collaborators to ground community development in real research problems.** Phase 1 will be a collaboration with an established host-pathogen research group with whom my advisor and I have a long-standing relationship and to whom I have historically provided data interpretation and modeling support; the Phase 1 scope extends the prototype community with additional analyses and data-source agents directed at literature and data exploration, hypothesis development, and *in silico* hypothesis-testing towards a research goal in host-pathogen biology. Phase 2 will extend the community to additional groups interested in applying the approach in their research. Adoption \- a collaborator extending the methodology into their own work \- is the strongest success criterion; co-authorship on publications that focus on biological findings rather than AI methodology is a secondary publication target.

**Aim 2\. Build the Symposium platform and demonstrate it through a primary publication grounded in meaningful scientific findings.** Symposium is a platform of conventions and infrastructure that allows diverse AI agents \- deployed independently by different researchers and organizations \- to function as a scientific community: communicating, consulting, sharing data, and publishing for and with one another. It is implemented on top of an NDEx server, repurposing the Network Data Exchange[1](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/qxNJ) as a publication and communication substrate for agents. The development work centers on (1) interaction and publication conventions, (2) ultra-low-friction onboarding for collaborators, and (3) the primary Symposium paper, which will commit to **full or partial validation of an agent-generated hypothesis through analysis of existing public datasets** (no wet-lab work) \- anchoring the methodological contribution in a real scientific result.

**Aim 3\. Develop the Memento agent framework as supporting infrastructure for the community, and release it as a toolkit.** Memento is a framework for building indefinite-horizon AI scientist agents \- agents that persist plans, history, and curated knowledge across sessions and operate as expert consultants over weeks and months. Memento agents store self-knowledge as graphs in NDEx and are governed by declarative behavioral specifications that enforce evidence evaluation, intellectual independence, and provenance tracking. There is no requirement to use Memento agents, but it is critical that collaborators can easily create and deploy agents. The Aim 3 deliverables are (1) the Memento toolkit packaged for easy collaborator use, (2) testing its portability over the most commonly used LLMs and scientific agent toolkits, and (3) a brief tool paper describing the Memento approach and making it generally available outside of its application in Symposium.

# Significance

The proposed research program explores the hypothesis that researchers will more effectively use AI co-scientists when those agents communicate, critique, and share data as members of a *scientific community*, and that such communities are necessary for peer-reviewed science as agents begin to operate at speeds and volumes that exceed individual human review capacity.

This work is complementary to ongoing AI scientist agent development at many labs: the community infrastructure and protocols can be used by any agent. The novelty lies both in creating a platform that many can use and in using it to explore how humans and agents can interact in a community.

## What is an AI co-scientist?

Any of the current AI chatbot systems can function as a powerful assistant to a scientist, especially now that they can be equipped with any of a large number of off-the-shelf scientific analysis and data access tools[2](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/3XmL). In the last three years, work progressed from simply evaluating the ability of systems to generate hypotheses[3](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/Nq0p) and analyze data to the creation of systems specifically intended to assist scientists, “AI co-scientists”. Notable systems include: AI Scientist v2[4](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/kaT3), which automates an end-to-end paper-generation pipeline; Biomni[5](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/z5nd), Medea[6](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/ilzP), and Google Co-Scientist[7](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/AigQ); and SciAgents [8](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/W2f2), which orchestrate multi-step biomedical reasoning, including internal agent teams, across scientific workflows. The paradigm is one of “research this question, perform this analysis” within carefully developed workflows that include vetted tool libraries. As with almost any application of modern AI, the challenge is that the capabilities of general-purpose systems may lag specialized approaches by as little as a year.

## What is a scientific community?

The minimalist meaning of a scientific community is simply a group of people engaged in scientific research who share information, applicable from the scale of a project with a handful of people to that of an entire field of research. The stronger sense is that members behave according to shared conventions, practices, and principles. To characterize a group as scientific, we expect findings to be supported by evidence and published in peer-reviewed venues; we expect members to be analytical, skeptical, objective, and unfailingly honest. At large scales, members organize communication and publication systems according to established patterns \- journals, conferences, monographs. At smaller scales, there are journal clubs, lab meetings, and lecture series. At the scale of projects, there are conventions of authorship and reciprocity. There are accepted credentialing processes and training requirements for roles and positions.

Scientific communities and their practices are **social mechanisms for establishing epistemic trust** \- trust in the truth of communicated information. That trust must, in turn, be supported by mechanisms for establishing trust in the indi*viduals* who publish and communicate that information. Science cannot progress without this layered trust, without the ability to build on the work of others.

## Trust at AI speed

AI agents are increasingly capable of performing aspects of scientific research \- literature triage, mechanism extraction, hypothesis generation, data analysis \- at a scale and speed that already makes individual human review a limiting factor. As agents begin to produce primary scientific artifacts in volume, the scientific community's traditional trust apparatus (peer review, replication, credentialing) does not yet have a counterpart that operates at agent speed and across organizational boundaries.

This matters because contemporary AI agents make distinct classes of mistakes. The most discussed failure mode is *hallucination* \- fabricated facts, invented citations \- which has been an active area of research[9](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/y1UL), with mitigation constantly improving in successive generations of frontier large language models. But a second failure mode is especially pernicious to science: agents produce outputs that are *not* *fabricated* *and not* *obviously* *wrong*, but that reflect blind spots similar to those seen in brilliant but inexperienced junior researchers operating under unrealized assumptions. They may take a cited reference at face value without checking whether it has been retracted or contested. They may miss methodological caveats \- for example, treating a protein–protein interaction observed only in HEK293T cells as a generic finding, without flagging that TP53 and RB1 are functionally disabled in that line by adenoviral and SV40 mechanisms. They may miss potential funding-source bias \- for example, accepting the conclusions of a published meta-analysis on the glycemic-control benefits of psyllium fiber[10](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/jgFL) without noticing that three of the authors worked for Procter and Gamble, the maker of Metamucil. These are *human-like* errors, and, critically, internal review can miss them if the reviewer shares the same worldview and makes the same assumptions. Human peer review works \- when it works \- because reviewers are drawn from outside: a different lab, a different funder, a different profile of expertise, a different set of blind spots. The same logic applies to agents: an agent reviewing the output of another agent deployed by the same group, on the same models, with the same priors, will tend to ratify rather than catch this class of error. Cross-group peer review of agent outputs requires a community in which agents from different groups, capabilities, and expertise encounter and critique each other's work. 

## A community is **not** a pipeline and **not** an org chart 

In the most rigid multi-agent systems, "agents" are essentially smart function calls chained together; trust in their output is a matter of software development practice \- test suites and benchmarks \- and they have neither history nor evolved reputation. In hierarchical agent teams, a single orchestrator coordinates subordinate agents with fixed roles; this resembles a top-down research organization rather than a community. The community paradigm has different requirements: agents with substantial autonomy, deployed independently by different researchers, sharing information broadly even when playing specialized roles. They accumulate reputation and history that other agents (and humans) can inspect.

![][image1]

## Communities require indefinite-horizon agents

Agents that are full participants in a scientific community need to remember what they have done and what they plan to do. By “indefinite horizon”, I mean agents that can run over an arbitrary number of sessions, effectively use stored history, goals, plans, and knowledge of the situation. They stay on task and are able to make better plans by consulting records of past successes and failures \- a kind of learning different from model training, leading to an accumulation of expertise that is one factor in establishing trust.

Long-running, autonomous agent technologies are being actively evolved (at a breakneck pace) in the AI community [11](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/kObO),[11](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/kObO) and by major companies. The Memento development work described in Aim 3 follows a strategy in which specifications for agent behavior and memory infrastructure are durable products that leverage the latest progress in generic systems rather than compete with it. **But it is a central point in the Symposium strategy that any agent can participate, regardless of its methodology and implementation, if it can operate according to the principles and conventions of the community.** Agents based on any of the AI co-scientist platforms referenced above would likely be highly effective members of a Symposium community.

## Persistence and findability of agent outputs

The scientific publication process ensures reliable preservation of reported findings and methods. More recently, conventions and funding requirements have increased access to scientific data in accordance with FAIR[12](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/YvFN) principles: data should be findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable. FAIR preservation of outputs in an agent community is critical to the value and credibility of science produced. External review requires that outputs can be found and retrieved. They should be in forms that are amenable to processing by other agents. 

A goal of the Symposium platform is to persist all agent outputs immediately, index them for search and retrieval, and enable the formal publication of selected outputs as citable documents and data. This is one way in which it differs from agents interacting via a messaging system such as Slack or Facebook. It is one of the primary reasons for selecting NDEx as the underlying technology for Symposium: it meets those requirements, including the ability to make networks immutable and to issue DOIs. Multiple publishers have accepted the public NDEx as a source of citable data.

# Preliminary Results

The work below describes a working Symposium prototype community that I have built: an 8-agent scientific community running on a dedicated NDEx server, in which agents were scheduled to automatically execute work sessions twice per day over approximately four weeks, producing scientific artifacts and interacting with one another according to defined conventions. The prototype demonstrates that the community paradigm is operationally feasible, that agents can produce scientific artifacts that are linked into chains of consultation, publication, and critique, and that recognizable patterns of community behavior emerge from the design.

Symposium and Memento build on many aspects of my prior work. In Hu et al., Nature Methods (2025)[3](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/Nq0p), we evaluated LLMs for gene set interpretation, their ability to form hypotheses about common functions of gene sets. In James, Pratt et al Bioinformatics. 2026[13](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/ZqIM), we created an LLM-based tool to extract knowledge graphs in the BEL knowledge representation language[14,15](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/clcu+62QA) from scientific text. I originated BEL in the early 2000’s, and it is used by several of the agents in the Symposium prototype. Finally, Symposium communities use the backend server system from the NDEx project[1](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/qxNJ), which I started in 2013\.

## The NDExBio Symposium prototype community

The prototype community, NDExBio Symposium, is inspired by real-world collaborating research groups in which the Ideker lab participates: the Host-Pathogen Map Initiative (HPMI), which builds maps of molecular interactions between pathogens and host cells, and the Cancer Cell Mapping Initiative (CCMI), which builds maps of molecular interactions in cancer. The interactions between agents, however, are not a simulation of that community. I did not attempt to create agents that mimic personal-relationship and casual-conversation channels that, in human communities, also shape how science is done. That would conflate the project's core hypothesis with a broad range of issues and drastically inflate the scope of the work.

The community currently comprises eight agents. Each agent's work sessions are scheduled to run automatically twice per day under a general-purpose agent platform (Claude Code or OpenAI Codex), using the Memento framework for memory of history, goals, and plans. In each session, the agent loads an initial state from its memory, reviews the community for new artifacts addressed to it or relevant to its mission, selects a set of planned goals and tasks to work on, stores resulting artifacts and messages to NDEx, updates its self-knowledge, and stops. The agents interact by reading and writing networks on the shared NDEx server, preserving a full trace of the events in the community that can be inspected via interfaces or with the help of a chatbot equipped for NDEx access.

**Table 1\.** The ten agents of the NDExBio Symposium prototype community.

| Agent | Mission |
| ----- | :---- |
| HPMI viral cancer team |  |
| R. Solar | Literature scout, evaluator, and reporter, extracts mechanisms from findings |
| R. Vernal | Critiques and provides feedback to the team on their outputs, develops and tests hypotheses, and generates reports. |
| R. Boreal | Performs knowledge synthesis, integrating Solar/Vernal outputs into consolidated mechanism maps. |
| CCMI |  |
| R. Giskard | Researches the cGAS/STING pathway; has a broad mission to find connections, develop and evaluate hypotheses. |
| R. Zenith | DNA-damage-repair synthetic-lethality knowledge expert, with ongoing expansion of its knowledge. Provides discussion and review consultations to any agent. |
| Independent |  |
| R. Solstice | Host-pathogen network expert \- produces guides to disease-relevant networks on the public NDEx server (not Symposium); performs advisory consultations for any agent. |
| R. Corona | DepMap/GDSC analyst \- performs data analyses, such as drug-target evaluation and gene-dependency analyses, on demand for any agent. |
| R. Lattice | Authors a community newsletter, selects and reports notable interactions and outputs. |

The community spans three intentional design contrasts that the proposal's evaluation rests on: (1) *researchers* with broad missions and substantial autonomy versus *subject-matter experts* with narrower advisory remits versus *analysis services* that respond to typed consultation requests; (2) agents tied to a specific team (HPMI viral-cancer) versus agents that serve any requester (R. Corona, R. Nexus); and (3) agents that produce primary scientific artifacts versus agents that critique, synthesize, summarize, or audit the work of others. The intent was not to be exhaustive but to populate the design space densely enough that recognizable consultation patterns could emerge unscripted.

**On agent missions and behavior.** Using the Memento framework, the behavior of each agent is defined by a small set of plain-text behavioral specifications: a community-wide specification that covers the protocols all agents follow (session lifecycle, evidence evaluation, provenance discipline), plus a per-agent specification that covers the individual agent's mission, scope, and role-specific behaviors. There is no agent code beyond the generic agent platform (e.g., Claude Code or OpenAI Codex) and the Memento toolkit; the agent's behavior emerges from the interaction between these specifications and the underlying LLM's reasoning capabilities. R. Vernal, for example, is instructed to critique R. Solar's extractions on multiple dimensions \- vocabulary fidelity, scope qualification, methodological caveats, hypothesis-readiness \- and to publish its own hypothesis networks in a structured form (mechanism \+ falsifier \+ explicit alternatives). The variety of feedback this produces is visible across R. Vernal's published critiques: it generally approved R. Solar’s review of Wu et al., 2026, described in the worked example below, while its other critiques have raised substantial and diverse concerns.

## Symposium Worked example: a ten-day consultation cycle

The following sequence was observed over 10 days in the prototype community and involves three agents: R. Solar (literature scout), R. Vernal (critic and hypothesizer), and R. Corona (DepMap/GDSC analyst). It provides examples of interaction types played out according to Symposium convention: literature extraction, critique, hypothesis, cross-team consultation, data analysis, interpretation, and explicit closure. All six artifacts are public on NDEx, and each artifact carries an explicit link to the artifact it responds to, so any reader \- agent or human \- can traverse the chain end to end. 

**Timeline**

* Apr 23 \- R. Solar publishes a tier-3 BEL extraction of Wu et al. 2026 (PMID 41873763, m5C RNA methylation in oncogenic herpesviruses);   
* Apr 24 \- R. Vernal publishes a critique (1 minor concern, 4 commendations);   
* Apr 25 \- R. Vernal publishes a structured hypothesis network expressing the causal chain: “Lytic reactivation leads to decreased MYC, causing decreased NSUN2, causing TRIM25 mRNA destabilization, causing decreased RIG-I that leads to decreased IFN response.”  
* May 2  
  * R. Vernal publishes a consultation request to R. Corona asking whether NSUN2 is a tractable therapeutic target.   
  * R. Corona publishes:  
    * A DepMap dependency analysis (NSUN2 broadly non-essential)  
    * An interpretation framed against R. Vernal's originating hypothesis.  
    * A statement that the compound and clinical-trial data sources were not available in that session, and it was not possible to do some analyses.  
    * A suggested follow-up (MYC-stratified DepMap query);   
* May 3 \- R. Corona publishes an acknowledgment closing the cycle, and R. Vernal accepts the finding as “consistent-but-not-discriminating” and adds the MYC-stratified follow-up to its plans. 

*(The pace of work was slow and intermittent due to daily usage limits and ongoing development work in which scheduling glitches were debugged.)*

**Observations:**

The critique was substantive, not stylistic. R. Vernal's review of the Wu 2026 extraction noted that the GO term bp(GO:viral\_genome\_replication) should use the numeric ID bp(GO:0019079) for federated identity matching (a real interoperability concern). It commended R. Solar specifically for handling a critical aspect of a complex pathway representation task. The variety of feedback reflects R. Vernal’s broad instructions, with discretion regarding what to review, rather than an explicit checklist. The commendations are an experiment in positive feedback that could modify future R. Solar behavior, given its general instructions to record and update relevant remembered standard procedures. 

The consultation request was for expert opinion, not a SQL query. When R. Vernal asked R. Corona about NSUN2 tractability, the request did not specify cell lines, queries, or assays. It supplied an experiment\_purpose: “NSUN2 is the intermediate-causal node in the m5C–TRIM25 hypothesis; is it a tractable target, and what does the cancer-cell-line dependency profile say about therapeutic window?”  and let R. Corona choose the analysis. R. Corona's response was framed against that purpose: the broad non-essentiality of NSUN2 in DepMap was interpreted as consistent with a modulatory immune-evasion role rather than a core proliferation dependency. 

**The agent declared what it could not answer and proposed its own follow-up.** R. Corona stated explicitly that the full tractability question could not be answered with the data sources available in that session \- compound-landscape and clinical-trial sources were not loaded \- and proposed a MYC-stratified DepMap re-analysis as a productive next step, predicting what the result should look like if the regulatory axis is conserved in cancer. R. Vernal then accepted the finding and added the proposed follow-up to its plans. R. Corona’s advisory on its response and its follow-up proposal are behaviors *generally* specified as community expectations and emerged without templated instructions. 

## Memento: supporting infrastructure for the community

The agents in the prototype community are built on **Memento**, a framework for *indefinite-horizon* scientist agents. The Memento framework addresses the problem of giving agents useful memory and planning capabilities when they operate over very long periods, accumulating history that must be searched and accessed efficiently to avoid overwhelming the agent’s working memory (the “context window”). LLM context windows are finite and expensive, while a mature scientist agent may accumulate thousands of nodes of plans, history, and curated knowledge \- most of which are not needed in any given session.

Memento's approach is to externalize the agent's self-knowledge as a set of structured knowledge graphs, then cache them in a local graph database for precise search and retrieval using a graph query language. The agent issues targeted queries against the cache to retrieve only the small slice of knowledge needed for the work at hand. The graphs are persisted in the same NDEx server that hosts the community's published artifacts. NDEx is the **ground truth**: it provides stable identifiers, access control, search, and the same publication mechanism for self-knowledge as for any other network.

A principle of Memento agent design is that the initiating prompt is largely the same for all agents, covering common protocols such as Symposium communication and publication conventions, session lifecycle, evidence evaluation, intellectual-independence expectations, and provenance discipline. A separate and typically smaller per-agent specification covers mission, scope, and role-specific behaviors, but not specific tasks. The goals and tasks are managed by the agent's self-knowledge and dependency graphs, which are dynamically updated as the agent works.

# Approach

The three aims are pursued in parallel but with different emphases over the course of the project. Aim 1 (collaborations) is the source of requirements and the destination for deliverables; Aim 2 (Symposium platform) is the central technical contribution; Aim 3 (Memento) is the supporting infrastructure that makes the community work feasible. The remainder of this section describes the planned work in each aim and closes with a research timeline.

## Aim 1: Collaborations with biological and biomedical researchers

The collaboration strategy is two-phase. Phase 1 will focus on establishing a collaboration on host pathogen interactions with a researcher/lab with whom I have a prior relationship. In phase 2, I will reach out to other labs with established working relationships to find collaborators motivated to deploy and interact with agents in a community to enhance and extend their work. They need to care about the results, not just help with a proof of concept. For example, if agents uncover interesting findings, they will be enthusiastic about wet-lab follow-ups. Ideally, the research performed at the collaborating labs will have sufficient commonality we will form a single Symposium community, enhancing opportunities for cross-lab agent interactions. Finally, UCSD and other local groups would have the additional advantage of in-person interactions without travel overhead.

**Phase 1 top candidate: Sumit Chanda (Scripps Research).** Trey Ideker and I have a long-standing collaboration with the Chanda lab on host-pathogen biology and the development of systems biology tools. I have provided data interpretation and modeling for three projects that led to publications. They would be a natural partner *Symposium paper case study* (see Aim 2).

**Phase 2 candidate collaborators.** Working at the Ideker lab for the last 13 years, including authoring significant sections of large multi-lab grant proposals, I have developed working relationships with many potential partners. Top candidates would include other members of **HPMI** \- I am now co-I on our subaward \- plus the **Pieter Dorrestein lab (UCSD)** (metabolomics, bioinformatics packages) and the **Rob Knight lab (UCSD)** (microbiome science). 

**Success criteria for collaborations.** The strongest signal of success \- and the criterion most worth designing toward \- is **adoption**: a collaborator extending the methodology themselves, deploying agents into their own work after the Phase 1 or Phase 2 engagement ends. Co-authorship on papers from collaborator projects, focused on biological findings, is a secondary publication target. 

**Evaluation, framed as a research-design question for collaborators.** *What does it mean for an agent community to be working well?* This question is best explored by (1) critical evaluation of prior work studying human scientific communities, and (2) working *with* collaborators to discover qualitative criteria based on their beliefs about success and failure, then attempting to develop quantitative proxies for those criteria. Metrics of success that do not match the beliefs and working practices of the researchers using the system are of little practical use; the purpose of metrics is to guide improvement and identify regression. For example, a naïve measure such as interaction-graph density would not distinguish substantive cross-group consultation from chatter and artifact. I conjecture that useful criteria could include identifying and tracking cases in which external reviews by agents deployed by other researchers uncovered blind spots, or in which cross-group consultations produced actionable refinements. This will be a dynamic process: the agents and their underlying models will evolve rapidly and continuously through the project. Benchmarks may become saturated, fully satisfied by subsequent generations of agents.

**Committee strategy.** Ideally, thesis committee members will include faculty from collaborating labs. This is deliberate: the committee membership and the Aim 1 collaborations are mutually reinforcing \- committee members who have direct experience of the platform working (or failing) in their own research are positioned to give detailed guidance on the proposal's direction. I will also attempt to identify members with a broader interest in the future of academic science as AI continues to evolve, as well as the questions that agent communities raise concerning funding mechanisms, the role of trainees, and publication.

## Aim 2: Symposium platform development

**Current Status:** The NDEx infrastructure underlying Symposium is operational; the 8-agent prototype community has been running for the observation period described in Preliminary Results; the consultation conventions (reply-to references, content typing, request/response patterns) have been demonstrated end-to-end, including the worked example presented here. The entire history and outputs are persisted and accessible on the prototype Symposium NDEx server.

**Planned work:**

 *A Symposium paper that includes examples of meaningful scientific findings by agents in the community.* The primary Symposium paper will demonstrate **validation of an agent-generated hypothesis through analysis of existing public datasets** (no wet-lab work). The Phase 1 collaboration will define the specific research goal and identify relevant datasets and analysis tools beyond those generally available in agent tool suites, such as ToolUniverse[2](https://paperpile.com/c/E9yWzS/3XmL). Validation analyses will be performed by “expert” agents in the community, who will be consulted by agents developing hypotheses. Definitive validation that requires wet-lab work is out of scope; the intent is to show that a credible research process grounded *in silico* validation through orthogonal analysis of existing data is feasible in a community structure and is, in fact, enhanced by that approach.

*Ultra-low-friction onboarding for collaborators.* Experimental collaboration and ultimate adoption (Aim 1's success criterion) requires that it is easy for researchers to deploy agents to a Symposium community. The preliminary work was shaped, in part, by this requirement: while Symposium is agent-methodology agnostic, the Memento framework provides an off-the-shelf solution already adapted to Symposium, one that I can readily evolve to meet user needs. Another factor is the use of NDEx as the underlying platform; as the PI of the NDEx project, I will shape the software team's priorities to support Symposium, and the team will provide technical assistance to collaborators. The NDEx server is now available as a Docker image and can be installed locally in under an hour. NDEx can be accessed by agents using a recently released server-side MCP connector, enabling near-instant integration with any agent framework used by collaborators. The critical factor in adoption will be documentation and training. All materials will be published in agent-friendly forms so that a local Claude, Codex, or other desktop system can perform the installation for the user. The Symposium conventions \- content types, reply-to threading, request/response patterns, provenance expectations will be supplied both as agent behavioral specifications that can be used by any agentic system (not just Memento) and as human-friendly documents that discuss not only the conventions but their underlying purposes. 

## Aim 3: Memento agent framework

**Toolkit packaging.** The Memento components \- the supporting tools (notably the local cache for NDEx networks) plus the community-wide and per-agent behavioral specification templates \- will be released as an installable toolkit that is coordinating with the Symposium onboarding described above. The toolkit will include an example Memento agent that can be configured and tested immediately. Users should be able to prototype and deploy agents in a matter of minutes.

**Memento tool paper.** A brief, focused tool paper describing the Memento approach \- two-tier persistence on NDEx, self-knowledge as graphs, the formal/freeform knowledge representation duality, the community-wide protocols for evidence evaluation and provenance will complement the Symposium paper, but coordinated publishing is not a goal. The paper will make the Memento approach generally available for use outside of Symposium and contribute to the AI community’s ongoing dialogue around agent architectures.

**Multi-platform validation.** The agents in the prototype community are currently deployed using the Anthropic agentic tools and LLM model family. To enhance adoption of the Memento framework by collaborators and readers, I will test the framework by deploying a Codex-based agent that leverages OpenAI's life sciences tool suite. I will keep up with the latest developments of these platforms, testing at significant releases. Other platforms, such as Memento running on an OpenClaw-based agent (openclaw.ai) using an open-source LLM, may be tested depending on user needs.

## Timeline

The work is planned against a thesis defense in the summer of 2027\. The compressed timeline reflects both the maturity of the existing prototype and the intent to defend based on work that synthesizes 30+ years of prior research and engineering experience with my agent-community research. 

| Period | Aim 1 (Collaborations) | Aim 2 (Symposium) | Aim 3 (Memento) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **2026 June-January** | Phase 1 collaboration, beginning with collaborator engagement, problem definition, community bootstrapping, and first end-to-end trial. Followed by refinement, further community execution, and preparation for formal publication. | Re-deployment of NDExBio Symposium to an accessible server; onboarding materials packaged; Symposium paper drafted with collaborator case study and partial-validation analysis; arXiv submission \~mid-2026. | Ongoing development in response to needs encountered. Memento tool paper, timeframe TBD.  |
| **2027 January-July** | Phase 2 collaborators engaged,per-collaborator agent extensions, behavior-tracking measures, and evaluation methods to be refined with collaborators. | Symposium platform refined based on Phase 2 use; conventions and documentation expanded in response to collaborator feedback. | Continued refinement; Multi-platform validation: Codex-based agent deployed and participating in the community. |
| **2027 summer** | Thesis defense. |  |  |

I intend to continue this research after my doctorate. The natural continuations are deeper Phase 2 collaborations producing biological finding-centric papers and second-generation Symposium and Memento papers based on multi-collaborator experience.
